title: Source lineage for A Fourteenth Century English Biblical Version
summary: The repository XML is a normalized copy of an exact legacy U-M package encoding Paues’s 1904 edited selection. The 1904 text revises a privately printed 1902 thesis predecessor and combines differentiated evidence from five witnesses: S for the Prologue, Epistles, and Matthew; C for Acts; P for collation; D through Haggitt’s loaned transcript for Appendix I and other readings; and H for Appendix II variants. No single witness contains the complete cataloged selection. Digital facsimile, catalog, legal-support, access, and rights layers are modeled separately from historical textual transmission.
review_notes: Repository-to-raw derivation is supported/high, not confirmed, because semantic equivalence is exact under documented normalizations but the transformer is undocumented.
The exact raw package and conceptual current U-M item are distinct entities; no current serialization was byte-compared.
The 350-image U-M package is complete for the provider’s printed block, not the physical binding.
Parker MS 434 is the only witness with a verified complete public provider facsimile in this research.
Catalog records, MODS/TEI metadata, the PIMS checklist, and Copyright Office guidance are descriptive/support layers outside the textual chain.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for ajg4507
entities.description: The exact XML artifact consumed through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME submodule.
notes: The file has 12,162 elements and retains the raw U-M text and hierarchy while adding NODE to 167 division elements, ID DLPS1–DLPS3018 to all NOTE1 elements, and changing EDITORIALDECL N from 1 to 4.
No transformer or ingest log documenting these repository normalizations was located.

entities.label: Raw U-M book package for AJG4507.0001.001
entities.description: The exact legacy U-M package redistributed by Internet Archive: embedded XML and text, 350 TIFF page images, header, pageview data, and load log.

date_statements.label: Package creation
date_statements.notes: Embedded header date 2006; the raw files preserve May 2006 mtimes.

entities.reproduction_format: ZIP package containing XML, TXT, HDR, logs, pageview data, and 350 TIFF G4 page images
notes: The package is complete for its 350-image provider sequence and printed block, not for the physical binding: no covers, boards, spine, or pastedowns are represented.
The embedded XML is 854,336 bytes with SHA-256 216c0e26e2967f0cb676e9120ce0fd39e5f7cf5256235f0c241980c0113ab3.

entities.label: Current public U-M item AJG4507.0001.001
entities.description: The conceptual current U-M digital-text presentation identified by the persistent UMDL name URL. No current downloadable serialization was byte-compared in this research.
notes: This entity is separated from the exact legacy raw package so a current provider presentation is not silently equated with 2006 bytes.

entities.label: U-M 350-image page carrier for AJG4507.0001.001
entities.description: The photographed printed-book carrier represented by 350 contiguous 600 dpi TIFF images in the raw U-M package.
entities.reproduction_format: 350 unique 3296 × 5088 pixel, 1-bit, Group 4 compressed TIFF images at 600 dpi
notes: Provider sequence 00000001–00000350 is complete and internally contiguous.
This completeness describes the provider’s photographed printed block only; it is not a claim that the bound physical object is fully represented.

entities.label: Paues’s 1904 edition of A Fourteenth Century English Biblical Version

bibliographic.citation: Anna C. Paues, ed., A Fourteenth Century English Biblical Version (Cambridge: University Press, 1904).
bibliographic.title: A Fourteenth Century English Biblical Version

notes: Cataloged selection: title matter; main edited text pp. 1–208; Appendix I pp. 209–225; Appendix II pp. 226–229.
The introduction and remaining front/back matter are evidentiary but omitted from the repository transcription.

entities.label: Paues’s privately printed 1902 Upsala thesis edition
alternate_labels: A fourteenth century English Biblical version: consisting of a prologue and parts of the New Testament

bibliographic.citation: Anna C. Paues, A Fourteenth Century English Biblical Version: Consisting of a Prologue and Parts of the New Testament (Cambridge: University Press, 1902).
bibliographic.title: A Fourteenth Century English Biblical Version: Consisting of a Prologue and Parts of the New Testament

notes: Private doctoral-thesis predecessor containing the introduction and main Prologue/New Testament text through printed p. 208, but not the 1904 appendices.
Paues’s 1904 preface distinguishes the revised public edition and says its introduction is special to itself.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Paues’s 1904 edition
entities.reproduction_format: 366-page Text PDF with item metadata and Scribe scandata

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Paues’s 1902 thesis edition
entities.reproduction_format: 292-page Text PDF with item metadata and Scribe scandata

entities.label: Cambridge, Selwyn College, MS 108.L.1 (S)

date_statements.label: Current MWM date
date_statements.value: Beginning of the fifteenth century (s. xv in)

holding.notes: Owned and cataloged under Selwyn College’s shelfmark; Cambridge University Library states that Selwyn’s medieval manuscripts are deposited at CUL and may be consulted in its Special Collections Reading Room.

notes: Current MWM catalog describes one Anglicana Formata scribe; Paues had assigned ff. 86r–101v to a second hand.
Main collection at ff. 1r–139r: Prologue, Catholic and Pauline Epistles, Acts, and Matthew 1:1–6:13.
Paues used S as base for the Prologue, Epistles, and Matthew, but not Acts.
MWM assigns Herefordshire scribal dialect to the Prologue and Epistles; this is not automatically a place of composition.

entities.label: Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, Parker Library MS 434 (P)

date_statements.label: Paues’s date
date_statements.value: Earlier half of the fifteenth century
date_statements.notes: Retained as a conflicting historical assessment.

notes: 22 lines per page.
Relevant New Testament text is cataloged at manuscript pp. 3–319.
Contains the main Prologue/Epistles/Acts/Matthew collection, but not Paues’s appendices.
Paues argues P was copied from S after S’s first corrector and uses P principally for collation; the current catalog date creates a chronology requiring review.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Dd.12.39 (C)

notes: About 30 lines per page.
Independent Middle English Acts occupies ff. 16–72.
Paues’s oldest and principal Acts witness, described as closest to the original.
The manuscript also contains other texts outside this cataloged selection.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Douce 250 (D)

date_statements.label: Current Bodleian catalog date
date_statements.value: Second half of the fourteenth century

notes: Contains parts of a Middle English New Testament at ff. 1r–86v.
Appendix I prints its distinct James, 1 Peter, 2 Peter, and 1 John versions; main-text overlaps were collated.
The edition’s textual access is explicitly mediated by the Haggitt transcript.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Holkham misc. 40 (H)

date_statements.label: Current Bodleian catalog date
date_statements.value: First quarter of the fifteenth century

physical_description.support: Parchment of mediocre quality, with paper flyleaves
notes: Relevant General and Pauline Epistles occupy current catalog ff. 133r–162va.
Five scribes overall; the relevant Epistles are assigned to the fourth scribe.
Paues discovered H after the main text had been printed and supplied its variants in Appendix II.
The present catalog’s endpoint ‘162va’ is retained literally pending foliation review.

entities.label: Haggitt working transcript of Bodleian MS Douce 250
alternate_labels: Transcript of D loaned to Anna Paues
notes: Modeled provisionally as a manuscript_witness because it is a distinct handwritten or typed textual intermediary, not because it is medieval.
Its maker’s full identity, material form, scope, location, survival, and whether Paues also consulted D directly are unresolved.

notes: The complete provider object has 336 canvases; the target main collection occupies canvases 11–329, labeled manuscript pp. 3–319.

CUL’s deposit statement is collection-level but expressly covers Selwyn College’s medieval manuscripts, the class to which MS 108.L.1 belongs.
No complete public image facsimile was located.

access.access_method: Submit CUL’s imaging and permissions request form, choosing private-study copies or the appropriate publication/commercial-use option. Item condition, deposited-item authority, quotation, and any owner approval remain subject to CUL review.

access.format: Item-specific JPEG or TIFF images on request
notes: CUL says it supplies a quote after reviewing the request; exact cost and coverage for MS 108.L.1 are therefore unknown.
Private-study supply and permission for publication or other reuse are distinct options; CUL’s image permission does not clear further underlying rights.
Whether deposited-item imaging additionally requires Selwyn College approval was not established.

access.access_method: Email Selwyn Library only to clarify the owner-side approval, attribution, or reuse requirements for its deposited manuscript; physical consultation is through CUL, not this route.

rights.component: Pinned CME XML serialization, including contributor-owned repository additions and inherited U-M transcription and markup
rights.jurisdiction: Repository license assertion; inherited-source scope unresolved
rights.basis: The pinned CME root LICENSE is the Unlicense and repeatedly scopes its dedication to ‘software’; it does not expressly identify inherited U-M XML data, transcription, or markup. The assertion is retained without treating it as authority to relicense upstream material.

rights.component: Underlying medieval Middle English text and Paues’s 1904 editorial text
rights.basis: The medieval text is centuries old and the 1904 edition was published more than 95 years ago; current U.S. term guidance supports public-domain status. This does not decide provider terms, foreign jurisdictions, or modern markup.

rights.component: Any General Corpus-generated output and project-owned additions derived from this source
rights.jurisdiction: Project output license; underlying public-domain matter excluded
rights.basis: The General Corpus root README states that all output except public-domain matter is licensed CC BY-SA 4.0. This downstream output policy is recorded separately and does not override the source XML, provider terms, or public-domain status.

rights.component: Legacy raw package, embedded OCR/XML, metadata, and delivery archive
rights.basis: The embedded legacy header says ‘Where applicable, subject to copyright’ and warns that other distribution restrictions may apply; the raw Internet Archive metadata supplies no open license. No current U-M rights statement is projected backward onto these exact raw bytes.

rights.component: Current U-M digital-text presentation
rights.basis: The current U-M item displays the standardized No Copyright–United States statement. That provider assertion is recorded as a rights statement, not a license and not a worldwide public-domain determination.

rights.component: Current U-M page-image presentation
rights.basis: The current U-M item applies No Copyright–United States at item level; this record keeps that assertion distinct from the legacy raw package warning and does not treat it as an open license.

rights.component: Exact 350 TIFF files in the legacy raw ZIP
rights.basis: The raw embedded header carries a generic copyright/restrictions warning and the raw package metadata has no license. The current item-level NoC-US statement is not treated as a retroactive license for exact legacy files.

rights.component: 1904 edition text, introduction, apparatus, and typographical arrangement
rights.basis: First published in 1904, the edition is beyond the maximum U.S. term described in current Copyright Office guidance.

rights.component: 1902 privately printed thesis edition text and arrangement
rights.basis: The edition was printed and circulated in 1902 and is beyond the maximum U.S. term described in current Copyright Office guidance.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan images, OCR derivatives, PDF container, metadata, and scandata
rights.basis: Internet Archive metadata reports NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for the 1904 publication in the United States, but supplies no item-specific open license for scan images, OCR derivatives, or delivery files. Underlying edition status is recorded separately.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan images, OCR derivatives, PDF container, metadata, and scandata
rights.basis: Internet Archive metadata reports NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for the 1902 publication in the United States, but supplies no item-specific open license for scan images, OCR derivatives, or delivery files. Underlying edition status is recorded separately.

rights.component: Underlying medieval manuscript text
rights.basis: The vernacular text predates modern copyright by centuries. This statement does not affect ownership of the physical manuscript, reproduction permissions, modern cataloging, or image rights.

rights.component: Images or copies supplied by Cambridge University Library and permission for their requested use
rights.basis: CUL separates private-study image supply from publication and other reuse, requires its licence for use of CUL-supplied images, and states that its permission does not clear further underlying rights. No item-specific quote, licence, or Selwyn-owner approval for MS 108.L.1 was obtained.

rights.component: Underlying medieval manuscript text
rights.basis: The vernacular text predates modern copyright by centuries. This statement does not affect ownership of the physical manuscript, reproduction permissions, modern cataloging, or image rights.

rights.component: Modern transcript’s selection, transcription, and material expression
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and unknown current holding
rights.basis: The transcript was made no later than 1904, but its maker’s identity, date of death, publication history, location, and rights history are unresolved; unpublished-work and foreign-jurisdiction rules may apply.

rights.component: PIMS microfilm reproduction of historical Holkham Hall MS 672
rights.basis: The checklist supports an onsite microfilm access route, but no reel-specific reproduction license or reuse permission was verified; underlying medieval text and the physical manuscript remain separate components.

rights.component: Stanford/Parker manuscript images
rights.basis: The official MODS record expressly licenses the images under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International and directs commercial or publication-quality requests to the Parker Library.

rights.component: Modern MWM catalog description and HTML presentation
rights.basis: The page footer asserts copyright © 2009 University of Birmingham; no open license was found.

rights.component: MODS metadata record and XML delivery file
rights.basis: The record supplies a CC BY-NC license for manuscript images, not an explicit license for the MODS metadata or XML file itself.

rights.component: 1856 catalog text and arrangement
rights.basis: The catalog was published in 1856 and is beyond the maximum U.S. term.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan and PDF delivery carrier
rights.basis: No item-specific open license for the scan images or PDF carrier was verified; the public-domain status of the underlying 1856 catalog is recorded separately.

rights.component: Modern Bodleian catalog description and TEI XML
rights.basis: The pinned repository file and current record were verified, but no file-specific open license was found in the pinned TEI or repository root during this research.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Five witnesses with different textual scopes and states
editorial_practices.representation: S as base for Prologue, Epistles, and Matthew; C as base for Acts; P, D transcript, and H used for collation or appendices
editorial_practices.description: Paues constructed the main text and appendices from explicitly differentiated witness roles rather than presenting one manuscript diplomatically.
editorial_practices.scope: Main pp. 1–208 and Appendices I–II pp. 209–229

editorial_practices.source_feature: Scribal errors, translator errors, lacunae, and variant readings
editorial_practices.representation: Obvious scribal errors corrected; translator errors corrected only when sense required; deviations noted; omissions common to all witnesses supplied without note
editorial_practices.description: The edition is interventionist and eclectic within rules stated in its introduction, not a purely diplomatic transcription.
editorial_practices.scope: Edited Middle English text and apparatus

editorial_practices.source_feature: Editorial additions and alternative or glossed readings
editorial_practices.representation: Square brackets for additions; parentheses for alternatives or glosses; Vulgate chapter and verse numbering and added headings
editorial_practices.description: Modern typographic conventions distinguish editorial intervention and explanatory alternatives.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Orthographic, phonetic, substantive, and late-discovered witness variants
editorial_practices.representation: Mere orthographic and inconsequential phonetic variants generally omitted; substantive deviations reported; Holkham variants moved to Appendix II after discovery
editorial_practices.description: The apparatus is selective and chronologically layered.
editorial_practices.scope: Notes throughout and Appendix II pp. 226–229

editorial_practices.representation: Uncorrected OCR with Level 1 TEI-in-Libraries encoding, page-image links, and omitted front/back matter represented chiefly by PB/EPB wrappers
editorial_practices.description: The raw header states that no OCR corrections or content editing were performed; the electronic text selects title, main text, and appendices while linking all 350 images.

editorial_practices.representation: Same normalized hierarchy and text, with 167 NODE attributes, 3,018 NOTE1 IDs, EDITORIALDECL N=4, XML declaration changes, and whitespace reformatting
editorial_practices.description: Computational comparison isolates structural normalization from textual change, but the responsible transformer is undocumented.

evidence.citation: Computational structural, normalized-text, and normalized-attribute comparison of repository ajg4507.xml with raw-package embedded XML.
evidence.locator: all 12,162 elements; 15,659 normalized nonempty text nodes; complete TEXT; complete attribute inventory
evidence.summary: Both files have the same 12,162-element path sequence (SHA-256 a18cc898be77595129bf39d579553dd6e2e3267dd5942dedb5f3165549434691), normalized text-node sequence (def34164b70b3a9f8a5d5d7f6077cf03d7b5ac96eaefb2dbe6934aa8391006f5), and normalized TEXT (1f51585aa6b82bb731cc50e315daebb1eaebbc5677087edf86a80db2d1d623ea). Attribute differences are exactly 167 added NODEs, 3,018 added NOTE1 IDs, and EDITORIALDECL N 1→4, plus declaration/whitespace formatting.
notes: The compared raw embedded XML is 854,336 bytes, SHA-256 216c0e26e2967f0cb676e9120ce0fd39e5f7cf5256235f0c241980c0113ab3.

evidence.citation: Selwyn College, Selwyn Library.
evidence.locator: Old Library paragraph; opening-hours panel; Contact us panel
evidence.summary: Selwyn states that its Old Library contains the college’s archives, rare books, and special collections and gives library@sel.cam.ac.uk for library enquiries. Because CUL separately confirms that Selwyn’s medieval manuscripts are deposited there, this page supports only an owner-side clarification channel for MS 108.L.1, not its physical consultation venue or any reproduction or reuse grant.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, College collections deposited in the University Library.
evidence.locator: Other Colleges; Selwyn College and Ridley Hall
evidence.summary: Cambridge University Library states that Selwyn College’s medieval manuscripts have been deposited at CUL and may be consulted in the Special Collections Reading Room under the usual procedures. This establishes the consultation venue for medieval MS 108.L.1, while owner-side reproduction or publication approval remains separate.

open_questions.question: How many translation and compilation events produced the Southern nucleus and Midland additions, and what exact dates, places, and Latin exemplars underlie each component?
next_steps: Collate all five witnesses and relevant collateral biblical manuscripts.
Consult recent scholarship on Selwyn 108.L.1 and non-Wycliffite Middle English scripture.
Keep dialect, scribal origin, composition, compilation, and physical exemplar distinct.

open_questions.question: Who was Miss Haggitt, what did her Douce 250 transcript contain, did Paues also inspect D directly, and where does the transcript now survive?
next_steps: Search Paues, Upsala, Newnham, Cambridge University Press, and Bodleian archival records.
Do not replace the explicit transcript intermediary with a direct edition-to-D transcription claim.

open_questions.question: How should the current Parker MODS date of approximately 1375–1399 and MWM’s early-fifteenth-century date for S be reconciled with Paues’s conclusion that P was copied from corrected S and her earlier-half-fifteenth-century date for P?
next_steps: Obtain current palaeographical opinions and inspect both manuscripts at high resolution.
Reassess the dating intervals, direction of copying, and possibility of catalog legacy dating before normalizing one chronology.

open_questions.question: How should Paues’s second hand at S ff. 86r–101v be reconciled with Ker and the MWM catalog’s one-scribe description?
next_steps: Inspect the relevant Selwyn folios directly or in new high-resolution images.
Consult Ker 1977 and recent Selwyn scholarship.

open_questions.question: Are complete current public facsimiles or item-specific reproductions available for S, C, D, or H, what are their exact image rights and coverage, and does imaging deposited S require approval from Selwyn College in addition to CUL’s licence?
next_steps: Submit an item-specific CUL Imaging Services enquiry for MS 108.L.1 to establish available coverage, condition approval, quote, supplied format, and licence terms.
Ask CUL and, if directed, library@sel.cam.ac.uk whether Selwyn-owner approval is additionally required for reproduction or publication of this deposited manuscript.
Contact Cambridge University Library and Bodleian Special Collections about C, D, and H.
Recheck institutional digital catalogs and record exact provider-object coverage before adding facsimile entities.

open_questions.question: What transfer and transformation history connects the May 2006 raw U-M package, the current public U-M item, and the normalized repository XML?
next_steps: Seek U-M DLPS ingest/migration documentation and CME import history.
Download and byte-compare a current serialization if U-M exposes one.
Keep copied_from bounded to the byte-compared raw package and current U-M as a qualified version_of relation.

open_questions.question: How should the legacy raw-package copyright warning and current U-M No Copyright–United States statement be reconciled for exact raw XML/TIFF files and modern provider layers?
next_steps: Request U-M item-level rights provenance and effective-date documentation.
Do not treat NoC-US as a license or as a retroactive worldwide determination for legacy package bytes.

open_questions.question: How do Paues’s Holkham Hall 672 locator beginning at f. 132, the current MS Holkham misc. 40 item at ff. 133r–162va, and the PIMS microfilm sequence correspond exactly?
next_steps: Inspect the manuscript or complete microfilm and reconcile historical and modern foliations.
Verify whether the current TEI endpoint ‘162va’ is intentional and audit the PIMS film’s exact coverage.

open_questions.question: What copyright or unpublished-work rules apply outside the United States to Paues’s editions, the five medieval witness texts, the Haggitt transcript, and modern catalog/image layers?
next_steps: Obtain jurisdiction-specific legal review before redistribution beyond documented licenses.
Keep physical ownership, textual copyright, image rights, metadata rights, and provider contract terms separate.
